text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC
OFFICE VISIT NOTE — DISEASE PROGRESSION / TREATMENT TRANSITION

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Margaret Chen is a 64-year-old woman with stage IV (M1c) EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma who presents today for review of her most recent CT imaging, which shows interval disease progression while on pemetrexed maintenance therapy. She was referred for discussion of next-line treatment options and clinical trial eligibility.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Chen is a 64-year-old retired accountant and former smoker (25 pack-years, quit approximately 10 years ago) with a known history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her oncologic history is detailed below.

She was originally diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with stage IIIB (T3N3M0, AJCC 8th edition) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma following evaluation of an incidental left upper lobe mass and subsequent CT-guided core needle biopsy. Molecular profiling at that time (NGS from the primary tumor) demonstrated an EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation, TP53 p.R175H co-mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification, with no KRAS, MET, ALK, ROS1, RET, or ERBB2 alterations. TMB was low (4 mut/Mb). PD-L1 TPS was 15%.

Given the sensitizing EGFR mutation and unresectable N3 disease, a multidisciplinary tumor board recommended upfront EGFR-directed therapy rather than standard chemoradiation. She was started on osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily as definitive first-line therapy. She achieved an excellent initial partial response, with the primary mass shrinking from 4.2 cm to a nadir of 1.5 cm at 9 months, with near-complete resolution of mediastinal and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. She tolerated osimertinib reasonably well, experiencing grade 2 acneiform rash managed with topical clindamycin and oral doxycycline, as well as grade 1 paronychia of the left index finger treated with warm soaks.

Unfortunately, at approximately 14 months into osimertinib therapy, she developed radiographic progression with an increase in the left upper lobe mass (1.5 cm to 2.8 cm), new left hilar adenopathy, and a new right adrenal nodule. Concurrent brain MRI revealed three new enhancing brain metastases (right frontal 1.4 cm, right parietal 0.8 cm, left cerebellar 0.6 cm), upstaging her disease to stage IV M1c. She was neurologically intact throughout. CT-guided biopsy of the right adrenal metastasis confirmed metastatic lung adenocarcinoma; repeat NGS on the adrenal specimen revealed acquisition of an EGFR T790M (exon 20) resistance mutation (AF 31%) as well as a new PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (AF 19%), on the background of persistent EGFR L858R, TP53 R175H, CDKN2A deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification.

She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to all three brain metastases using the Gamma Knife Perfexion platform (right frontal: 20 Gy x1, right parietal: 20 Gy x1, left cerebellar: 18 Gy x1) approximately 3 weeks after progression was documented. She had anticipated post-SRS grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 headache, managed with a short dexamethasone course. Whole-brain radiation was deferred in favor of SRS to preserve neurocognitive function.

Osimertinib was discontinued given clear acquired resistance. Second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin (AUC 5 IV day 1) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m² IV day 1) every 21 days was initiated approximately 6 weeks after progression documentation, once she had recovered from SRS. Vitamin B12, folic acid, and dexamethasone premedication were given per standard pemetrexed protocol. Cycle 2 was complicated by grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.7 x 10⁹/L), requiring a 1-week treatment delay and a 5-day course of filgrastim. G-CSF prophylaxis was added for all subsequent cycles. She completed a total of 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed induction with best systemic response of stable disease (primary mass stable at 2.8 cm, adrenal nodule stable at 1.1 cm, no new systemic lesions). Brain MRI after 3 cycles showed stable post-SRS treatment effect at all three prior sites with no new intracranial disease.

Following completion of 4 cycles of induction, she transitioned to pemetrexed maintenance (500 mg/m² IV every 21 days) with continued vitamin B12 and folic acid supplementation. She tolerated maintenance reasonably well without significant new toxicities.

Her most recent staging CT (performed approximately 3 weeks ago, after completing 3 cycles of pemetrexed maintenance) has now demonstrated interval disease progression, which is the primary impetus for today's visit. Imaging showed enlargement of the left upper lobe mass from 2.8 cm to 4.0 cm, growth of the right adrenal nodule from 1.1 cm to 2.0 cm, a new hepatic lesion in segment VI measuring 0.9 cm (new from prior), and a new small right-sided pleural effusion. These findings are consistent with clear systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance.

Ms. Chen reports she has been feeling more fatigued over the past 4-6 weeks, rating energy at roughly 5-6/10. She notes mild right-sided chest discomfort, which she attributes to the pleural effusion — she describes it as a dull ache rather than pleuritic pain, not significantly limiting her activity. She denies shortness of breath at rest and reports only mild exertional dyspnea when climbing one flight of stairs. She has not noted any abdominal pain or right upper quadrant discomfort referable to the hepatic lesion. She remains neurologically intact: no headaches, no visual changes, no focal weakness, no gait difficulties, no seizure activity. She continues her home medications including metformin for her type 2 diabetes and amlodipine and lisinopril for hypertension, all at stable doses.

She lives with her husband, who is actively involved in her care and accompanied her today. She maintains a good appetite with slight weight loss (~2 lbs over the past month), which she attributes to reduced appetite. She has been researching clinical trial options and came to this visit prepared to discuss enrollment.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

(+) Fatigue — mild to moderate, not disabling
(+) Mild exertional dyspnea (one flight of stairs)
(+) Right-sided chest discomfort — dull, non-pleuritic, new over past 3-4 weeks
(+) Decreased appetite, mild weight loss (~2 lbs)
(+) Occasional loose stools (1-2x/week), not disabling; attributed to metformin
(-) Hemoptysis
(-) Cough productive of purulent sputum (has a dry, intermittent cough, chronic)
(-) Orthopnea or PND
(-) Headache
(-) Vision changes, diplopia, or photophobia
(-) Focal neurological symptoms: weakness, sensory changes, speech difficulty, gait ataxia
(-) Nausea or vomiting
(-) Abdominal pain or right upper quadrant tenderness
(-) Jaundice or dark urine
(-) Bone pain
(-) Fever or chills
(-) Night sweats
(-) Peripheral edema
(-) Rash — prior osimertinib-related rash has fully resolved; skin intact
(-) Mouth sores (mild prior mucositis on pemetrexed, currently resolved)

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**
(Previously documented; unchanged)
1. Stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma (originally stage IIIB, upstaged to IV with brain/adrenal/liver/pleural metastases)
2. Hypertension — well controlled
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — on metformin, HbA1c last checked 3 months ago at 7.1%
4. Former smoker — 25 pack-years, quit ~10 years ago
5. Status post SRS x3 brain metastases

**SURGICAL HISTORY**
No prior thoracic or abdominal surgical procedures. No prior intracranial surgery.

**FAMILY HISTORY**
Father — colon cancer, age 71. Mother — type 2 diabetes. One sibling — no known malignancy. No family history of lung cancer, breast cancer, or BRCA-related malignancies.

**SOCIAL HISTORY**
Retired accountant. Married, lives with husband. Never used illicit drugs. Alcohol: occasional glass of wine, less than 1 drink/week during chemotherapy. Former smoker as above; no current tobacco use. Good social support.

**ALLERGIES**
No known drug allergies. No prior reactions to platinum compounds, pemetrexed, or contrast media.

**CURRENT MEDICATIONS**
1. Metformin 1000 mg oral twice daily (for type 2 DM)
2. Amlodipine 5 mg oral once daily (for hypertension)
3. Lisinopril 10 mg oral once daily (for hypertension)
4. Folic acid 400 mcg oral once daily (pemetrexed supplementation — plan to continue per trial protocol)
5. Vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks (last dose approximately 6 weeks ago; next dose due)
6. Omeprazole 20 mg oral once daily (GI prophylaxis while on dexamethasone premedication for pemetrexed)
7. Loperamide PRN (for loose stools)
8. Ondansetron 8 mg oral PRN (for nausea on pemetrexed days, rarely used recently)

Note: Dexamethasone (used as pemetrexed premedication) will be discontinued once pemetrexed is stopped. Short dexamethasone course for SRS-related cerebral edema has been completed.

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 128/76 mmHg
- Heart rate: 82 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- Temperature: 37.0°C (98.6°F)
- Oxygen saturation: 95% on room air
- Weight: 61.2 kg (baseline at diagnosis: 63.5 kg; loss of ~2.3 kg from peak)
- Height: 162 cm
- BMI: 23.3 kg/m²
- Performance status: ECOG 1-2 (able to perform light activity, up and about >50% of waking hours, but activity limited by fatigue)

General: Pleasant, well-groomed woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x4. Appears mildly fatigued but engaged and conversational. No cachexia.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact. No icterus, no conjunctival pallor. No lymphadenopathy in anterior or posterior cervical chains. Oropharynx clear; no mucositis or oral lesions at this time.

Neck: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy. The previously documented left supraclavicular adenopathy, which had resolved on osimertinib, remains clinically absent on exam today. Trachea midline.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Mildly decreased breath sounds at the right base consistent with small pleural effusion seen on CT. No egophony. Left lung fields with faint decreased air entry at the left apex over the area of the known primary mass. No wheeze or stridor. No dullness to percussion above the right base.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender. Liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below the right costal margin; no significant hepatomegaly. Spleen not palpable. No rebound or guarding. Normoactive bowel sounds.

Musculoskeletal/Extremities: No focal bone tenderness on palpation of the spine, ribs, or long bones. No clubbing. No cyanosis. Trace bilateral lower extremity pitting edema (1+), new from prior.

Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation grossly intact. Coordination with finger-nose and heel-shin testing intact. Normal gait. Romberg negative. No focal neurological deficits. No signs of cerebellar dysfunction.

Skin: Skin clear and intact. The prior grade 2 acneiform rash from osimertinib has completely resolved. No paronychia noted. No active rash.

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS** (most recent, from approximately 1 week prior to this visit)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.8 x 10⁹/L (reference 4.0–11.0)
- ANC: 3.2 x 10⁹/L (recovered; previously grade 3 nadirs during induction)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia; consistent with chemotherapy-related anemia; baseline pre-chemo ~12.5 g/dL)
- Platelets: 198 x 10⁹/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.78 mg/dL; estimated GFR >60 mL/min/1.73m²
- Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL
- AST: 32 U/L (upper limit of normal)
- ALT: 38 U/L (upper limit of normal — slightly elevated from prior, possibly related to hepatic lesion or metformin; will monitor)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 112 U/L (mildly elevated above baseline)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL

Other:
- Fasting glucose: 138 mg/dL (improved from earlier hyperglycemia while on pemetrexed; metformin dose currently 1000 mg BID)
- HbA1c (3 months ago): 7.1%
- LDH: 256 U/L (mildly elevated; may reflect tumor burden)
- CEA: 18.4 ng/mL (elevated; baseline pre-osimertinib was 22.1 ng/mL, nadir on osimertinib was 4.3 ng/mL, now rising again)
- Coagulation studies: PT/INR and PTT within normal limits

---

**IMAGING RESULTS**

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (approximately 3 weeks ago, see separate report for full details):
The left upper lobe mass has grown from 2.8 cm to 4.0 cm compared to the prior CT 3 months ago. The right adrenal nodule has grown from 1.1 cm to 2.0 cm. A new hepatic lesion in segment VI now measures 0.9 cm, which was not present on prior imaging. There is a new small right-sided pleural effusion. No new osseous or thoracic lymph node disease identified. Overall appearance is consistent with systemic disease progression while on pemetrexed maintenance.

MRI Brain with and without gadolinium: Ordered today; pending results at the time of this note. Will be reviewed urgently given second systemic progression; patient instructed to call with any neurological symptoms in the interim.

---

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

Ms. Chen is a 64-year-old woman with stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, now with acquired EGFR T790M resistance and a co-acquired PIK3CA E545K activating mutation, presenting with clear radiographic evidence of systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance therapy — her third line of treatment overall. Disease burden has expanded to include enlarging pulmonary primary, enlarging adrenal metastasis, a new hepatic lesion, and a new small pleural effusion. Intracranial disease remains controlled per prior brain MRI at last assessment, with stable post-SRS treatment changes and no new brain metastases identified; pending today's new MRI brain for confirmation.

**Problem 1: Systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance — transition to next-line therapy**

Her molecular profile, specifically the co-existence of an acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation alongside a PIK3CA E545K activating mutation, places her in a biologically distinct and clinically challenging resistance population. Standard third-line options for EGFR-mutant NSCLC after platinum-based chemotherapy are limited; her prior T790M-mediated resistance to osimertinib, which would have otherwise been a logical choice in the platinum-naive T790M+ setting, complicates straightforward re-challenge strategies. Given the PIK3CA co-mutation, PI3K pathway activation is a plausible driver of both the acquired osimertinib resistance and the relative modest response to platinum doublet chemotherapy.

Following a thorough discussion of available options — including docetaxel monotherapy, docetaxel/ramucirumab, further clinical trial enrollment, or best supportive care — and having reviewed her prior treatment history, molecular profile, and current performance status, I discussed in detail the availability of a Phase I/II clinical trial (protocol NCT-XXXXXX) evaluating a novel combination of an investigational EGFR inhibitor designed to overcome T790M/C797S resistance and a PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor (alpelisib analog) in patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC harboring acquired PIK3CA co-mutations. Ms. Chen has been made aware of the investigational nature of this therapy, the relevant risks including hyperglycemia (expected class effect of PI3K inhibition), potential for rash, mucositis, diarrhea, and hepatotoxicity, as well as uncertainty about efficacy.

Ms. Chen has expressed strong interest in clinical trial enrollment. She has reviewed the consent form with me and with her husband today, asked thoughtful questions, and signed the informed consent document. She meets eligibility criteria based on her molecular profile (EGFR L858R + T790M + PIK3CA E545K), prior therapy history, ECOG PS 1-2, and adequate organ function as confirmed by today's labs.

Plan:
- Pemetrexed maintenance: DISCONTINUED as of today.
- Enroll on Phase I/II trial, investigational EGFR inhibitor + PI3K inhibitor combination arm. Cycle 1 Day 1 to be scheduled at the trial site within 1-2 weeks, pending confirmation of baseline trial assessments.
- Baseline trial assessments to complete prior to C1D1: fasting glucose, HbA1c, LDH, hepatic function panel (already obtained this week — confirm values meet eligibility thresholds), ECG (QTc 438 ms on most recent ECG from pemetrexed cycle — acceptable; will recheck at baseline), ophthalmologic exam (trial requirement for PI3K inhibitor), and formal ECOG PS documentation.
- Vitamin B12 and folic acid: Will continue per trial protocol requirements given prior pemetrexed use.
- Metformin: Continue at current dose (1000 mg BID); diabetes management will be critically important given anticipated PI3K inhibitor-related hyperglycemia. Patient counseled extensively on dietary modification, low-carbohydrate diet, glucose monitoring at home, and when to call the clinic. Close coordination with her primary care physician and endocrinologist if needed.

**Problem 2: Brain metastases — surveillance**

MRI brain ordered today to confirm ongoing intracranial control prior to trial enrollment. Results pending; patient instructed to call with any neurological symptoms (headache, visual changes, confusion, weakness) and told we will call her with the MRI results, likely within 24-48 hours. If new or enlarging brain metastases are identified, urgent neurosurgery/radiation oncology co-management will be arranged. Trial eligibility requires stable CNS disease (no new or symptomatic brain metastases requiring urgent intervention). Prior SRS sites expected to show stable or evolving radiation changes.

**Problem 3: New right-sided pleural effusion — monitoring**

Small right-sided pleural effusion noted on CT. Patient is not dyspneic at rest, SpO2 95% on room air, and the effusion appears small on imaging. No thoracentesis needed at this time. Will monitor clinically; if she develops worsening dyspnea, decreased saturation, or the effusion enlarges on serial imaging, thoracentesis may be considered for both symptomatic relief and cytological confirmation of malignant etiology.

**Problem 4: Mild anemia**

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, consistent with chronic anemia of cancer/chemotherapy. Not symptomatic enough to warrant transfusion at this time. Will recheck CBC in the context of trial enrollment baseline labs. If further decline or symptomatic anemia (dyspnea at rest, fatigue significantly limiting activity), will revisit transfusion and/or ESA options per trial protocol restrictions.

**Problem 5: Mildly elevated hepatic transaminases**

AST 32 and ALT 38 (both at or just above the upper limit of normal), with mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase at 112. New hepatic lesion in segment VI raises the question of whether hepatic infiltration is contributing. Will monitor closely at trial baseline and subsequent visits. If significant worsening, hepatology consultation and trial eligibility re-assessment may be needed.

**Problem 6: Hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes — stable comorbidities**

BP well controlled today at 128/76. Fasting glucose 138 mg/dL, acceptable for now but glucose management will become a priority once PI3K inhibitor is initiated. Coordination with PCP requested. Will check HbA1c at trial baseline.

---

**PATIENT/FAMILY EDUCATION AND COUNSELING**

I spent approximately 45 minutes with Ms. Chen and her husband today in detailed discussion of:
- The meaning of disease progression on pemetrexed maintenance and the significance of the new lesions
- Her molecular resistance profile (T790M + PIK3CA co-mutation) and why this is clinically significant
- The rationale for transitioning to clinical trial versus standard third-line chemotherapy
- The investigational nature of the trial therapy, the structure of a Phase I/II trial, and expectations regarding response assessment
- The importance of glucose monitoring and dietary modification related to the PI3K inhibitor component
- Neurological symptom precautions pending MRI results
- Goals of care: at this time, the patient articulates her goal as continued treatment aimed at disease control; she is not currently interested in hospice or palliative care referral and maintains high functional independence. This conversation will continue to evolve with disease course.
- She was provided with written patient education materials about the trial, contact information for the research coordinator, and the clinic's 24-hour on-call line.

---

**FOLLOW-UP**
- MRI brain results: Will call within 24-48 hours
- Trial C1D1: Tentatively scheduled within 10-14 days, pending completion of all baseline trial assessments
- Next clinic visit: Day 14-15 of Cycle 1 for safety assessment and toxicity review
- Labs: Fasting glucose and CBC to be checked at C1D1 and Day 8 per protocol

Attending Physician: [Attending Thoracic Oncologist]
Electronic signature obtained at time of visit.
